question: The cost ledger scopes its $25/day circuit-breaker cap by a per-row 'day' string, currently the UTC calendar day (matches the TS original and all stored rows). Should day scoping stay UTC-day (fixed global boundary, stored rows stay directly comparable, matches OpenRouter's own reset clock) or move to host-local-day (matches the operator's intuitive 'today' but shifts meaning across hosts and breaks comparability of previously stored rows)? Whatever is chosen must keep existing stored YYYY-MM-DD values valid.
synthesis: Keep UTC-day scoping unchanged. All three advisors agree, the constraint is explicit ('existing stored YYYY-MM-DD values must stay valid'), and switching to host-local-day would create two incompatible semantic meanings in the same column for the same stored data — a direct violation of the stated requirement. UTC-day also maintains alignment with OpenRouter's own reset clock, so the circuit-breaker boundary and the upstream cost-reset boundary are identical, preventing double-counting edge cases. No code change is needed beyond confirming the existing behavior is intentional.
contradictions: Advisor 2 raises that UTC-day is 'wasted friction' in single-host single-timezone deployments, implying the benefit only materializes in distributed scenarios — but the other advisors treat UTC-day as unconditionally correct regardless of deployment topology. This tension is unresolved: the right answer depends on whether the system is ever actually deployed across time zones, which the question does not specify.

Advisors 3 and 1 frame the operator-confusion risk as a documentation problem; Advisor 2 frames it as a genuine ergonomic cost that may outweigh the consistency benefit in simple deployments. These are different risk assessments, not just different mitigations.
blindspots: DST and variable-length days: host-local-day was rejected here, but if it is ever reconsidered, 23- and 25-hour DST days would make the $25 cap inconsistent across days — UTC-day avoids this entirely and the advisors never named it as a reason.
NTP / system clock drift: host-local time is vulnerable to local clock skew; UTC sourced from a reliable time service is not. This is an additional silent argument for UTC that was not surfaced.

Operator UX near UTC midnight: operators in UTC-5 through UTC-12 will see the cap reset in the middle of their afternoon or evening, not at midnight. If the dashboard shows only the raw UTC date string with no local-time reset countdown, operators will routinely misjudge remaining budget. The advisors recommend documentation, but the actual failure mode is a surprise 429 during business hours — consider surfacing the UTC reset time explicitly in any operator-facing status output.

Row comparability assumption: the analysis assumes all existing rows were written with UTC-day semantics. If any deployment ever ran with a misconfigured local clock, those rows may already be inconsistent. A one-time audit of stored day values against known UTC timestamps would confirm the assumption before treating the column as authoritative.
